title: What are generics and TypeVar in Python?
answer: TypeVar defines a type variable so functions/classes can be generic over a type, letting static checkers preserve the relationship between input and output types.

title: What is __init_subclass__ in Python?
answer: __init_subclass__ is a classmethod hook that runs whenever a subclass is defined, letting a base class validate or register subclasses without a metaclass.

title: What is the difference between __repr__ and __str__?
answer: __repr__ gives an unambiguous, developer-facing representation (ideally re-creatable); __str__ gives a readable, user-facing one and falls back to __repr__.

title: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism in Python?
answer: Concurrency is structuring work to make progress on many tasks by interleaving (threads/asyncio); parallelism is running them literally at the same time (multiprocessing).

title: How does the asyncio event loop schedule coroutines?
answer: The event loop keeps a queue of ready tasks, runs each until it awaits, parks it on the awaited future, and resumes it via a callback when that future completes.

title: How does multiprocessing share data between processes?
answer: Processes have separate memory, so multiprocessing passes objects by pickling them over pipes/queues, or uses shared-memory primitives (Value/Array/shared_memory) for raw data.

title: What is an async iterator and async for?
answer: An async iterator defines __aiter__/__anext__ (a coroutine), and async for awaits each item, letting iteration suspend on I/O until StopAsyncIteration.

title: What is a race condition and how do locks prevent it?
answer: A race condition occurs when threads interleave on shared mutable state and corrupt it; a Lock serializes the critical section so only one thread mutates at a time.

title: What is a deadlock in Python?
answer: A deadlock is when threads each hold a lock the other needs and wait forever; avoid it by acquiring locks in a consistent global order or using timeouts.

title: How does heapq work in Python?
answer: heapq maintains a binary min-heap inside a plain list, giving O(log n) push/pop and O(1) access to the smallest element, ideal for priority queues and top-k.

title: How does the bisect module work?
description: Binary search on a sorted list.
answer: bisect does O(log n) binary search to find an insertion point in a sorted list, enabling fast lookups, sorted inserts, and range/grade queries.

title: How does collections.deque work internally?
answer: deque is a doubly-linked list of fixed-size blocks, giving O(1) appends and pops at both ends, unlike a list whose front operations are O(n).

title: How does an LRU cache work internally?
answer: An LRU cache combines a dict (O(1) lookup) with a doubly-linked list ordered by recency, moving hits to the front and evicting from the back when full.

title: How does Timsort make Python's sort fast and stable?
answer: Timsort finds existing sorted runs, extends them, and merge-sorts the runs; it is stable and adaptive, hitting O(n) on nearly-sorted data and O(n log n) worst case.

title: How do generator pipelines stream data lazily?
answer: Chaining generators forms a pull-based pipeline where each item flows through all stages on demand, processing huge or infinite streams with near-constant memory.

title: What is the difference between bytes and str?
answer: str is a sequence of Unicode code points (text); bytes is raw binary; you encode str to bytes and decode bytes back to str using a codec like UTF-8.

title: How does slicing with start:stop:step work?
answer: seq[start:stop:step] returns a new sequence from start up to (not including) stop, taking every step-th item; negative indices count from the end.

title: What is structural pattern matching (match/case)?
answer: match/case (3.10+) matches a value against structural patterns - literals, sequences, mappings, classes - binding variables on a match, more powerful than a switch.

title: What is a decorator factory (a decorator with arguments)?
answer: A decorator that takes arguments is a function returning a decorator returning a wrapper - three layers - so @repeat(3) configures behavior before wrapping the target.

title: How does exception propagation work up the call stack?
answer: An unhandled exception unwinds the call stack frame by frame, running finally blocks, until a matching except handles it or it reaches the top and crashes.

title: How does comparison chaining (a < b < c) work?
answer: Python expands a < b < c to (a < b) and (b < c) with b evaluated once, short-circuiting like the explicit and.

title: What is the difference between del, __del__, and garbage collection?
answer: del removes a name (dropping one reference); __del__ is a finalizer run when an object is reclaimed; the GC actually frees objects once unreachable, including cycles.

title: What is a weak reference in Python?
answer: A weakref points to an object without incrementing its refcount, so it doesn't keep the object alive; great for caches/observers that shouldn't prevent collection.

title: What is the difference between a generator and a coroutine?
answer: A generator produces values with yield for iteration; a native coroutine (async def) is awaited and suspends on I/O, driven by an event loop rather than next().

title: How does functools.reduce fold a sequence?
answer: reduce repeatedly applies a two-argument function across the sequence, carrying an accumulator, to fold many values into one (sum, product, max, etc.).
